posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked:
1947 Cos D’Estournel: St Estephe 2eme Cru Classe (Bordeaux, France)

I purchased this bottle as a bit of a gamble as it had no vintage on the label. The vendor guessed that it may be somewhere in the 1950’s. Olive green heavy glass bottle with a deep punt. Stood up for 24 hours, I carefully removed the cork as I guessed that the vintage may be stamped on it. I cannot tell you my emotions as I saw the number clearly marked ‘1947’ on the cork, one of the great Bordeaux vintages of the 20th century. Fortunately, I would be sharing this bottle with close family, so a number of us would enjoy my luck. The body is an opaque plum red and a deep red rim, which really surprises me. Smells of fine cooked beef wafts from my glass, as well as oxtail soup and a fine thread of sweet fruit. The mouthfeel is concentrated in flavour that just melts gently into my tastebuds. Fully resolved tannins, background acidity, such a velvet smoothness that I have only experienced in rare moments of wine greatness. It is humbling to think of how the world was in 1947 when this wine was made. Vintages can sometimes be bandied about with a carefree attitude and nonchalance.

Some news items (thank you Wikipedia) that provide further perspective:

In the UK (January 1947), the worst snowfall in the country in the 20th century, with extensive disruption of travel.

The Cold War begins: In an effort to fight the spread of Communism, US President Harry S. Truman signs an Act of Congress that implements the Truman Doctrine. This Act grants $400 million in military and economic aid to Turkey and Greece.

U.S. President Harry S. Truman signs the National Security Act of 1947 into law, creating the Central Intelligence Agency, the Department of Defense, the Joint Chiefs of Staff, and the National Security Council.

The United Nations General Assembly votes to partition Palestine between Arab and Jewish regions, which results in the creation of the State of Israel.

The IMF fund begins to operate.

The Muslim majority region formed by the Partition of India gains independence from the British Empire and adopts the name Pakistan.

The greater Indian subcontinent with a mixed population of Hindus, Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Jews, etc. formed by the Partition of India gain independence from the British Empire and retains the name India.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Beautiful post, thanks for sharing.

The depth of color is interesting, almost like an old brick where the red is present but tinged with some brown. I had some '47 Banyuls there were more on the brown side but delicious, and then this year a '47 Haut Bailly that was a pretty, translucent red.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That’s a nice surprise!

Sounds like classic old-school Cos. I worked through half a case of the 1961 a few years ago that had a similar color and similarly meaty, wild, exotic aromatics.

Did you give it a decant or just pop and pour?

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: Nice! Another poetic post of a lovely old bottle. Happy New Year!!

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the post Nicos.

I’ve only had the pleasure of a '47 Bordeaux once – a Chateau Margaux. An epic epic wine. It was served at a large group dinner and everyone got a small pour - maybe an ounce and a half. Late in the dinner it became clear that one of our table mates was a no-show. We split his pour four ways and savored every drop .

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked:
Thank you chaps.

William, I just popped and poured. This seems to work well.

My pleasure Rich.

posts.name: bob parsons alberta
posts.cooked: Very nice Nicos…have a great 2016. Maybe a London visit for me this year?

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked: Thanks Bob. Let me know if you are coming to London and I will choose a few mature bottles for us to drink.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Nicos. Happy New Year! 1947 Bordeauxs are so interesting. The acidity is crazy. Aromatically they are amazing because of this. Sounds great! I LOVE OXTAIL. Cheers.

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked: Don, a very Happy New Year to you too! This bottle was just so exotic and fascinating to drink over an evening. I do enjoy a good oxtail soup!

posts.name: François Audouze
posts.cooked:
What a wonderful surprise ! 1947 is a fantastic year with great wines in Bordeaux, the best being for me Lafleur, before Pétrus and Cheval Blanc.

Thank you for the nice story.

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked: Hello Francois, it was a happy surprise!
